outdated-wp-core.title: Outdated WordPress Core
outdated-wp-core.description: The WordPress core installation is running an outdated version with known security vulnerabilities.
outdated-wp-core.impact: Attackers can exploit known CVEs to gain unauthorized access, execute code, or steal data.
outdated-wp-core.fix_summary: Update WordPress core to the latest version immediately.
fix_steps: BACKUP your site (database + files) before updating
Option A (WP Admin): Go to Dashboard > Updates > Click 'Update to version X.X'
Option B (WP-CLI): Run 'wp core update' from the server command line
Option C (Manual): Download latest from wordpress.org, replace wp-admin/ and wp-includes/ directories
After updating, verify the site loads correctly and check for plugin compatibility

outdated-plugin.title: Outdated WordPress Plugin
outdated-plugin.description: One or more WordPress plugins are running outdated versions with known vulnerabilities.
outdated-plugin.impact: Vulnerable plugins are the #1 attack vector for WordPress sites. Attackers can exploit known plugin CVEs for remote code execution, SQL injection, or privilege escalation.
outdated-plugin.fix_summary: Update the affected plugin(s) to the latest version.
fix_steps: BACKUP your site before updating
Option A (WP Admin): Go to Plugins > select outdated plugin > Click 'Update Now'
Option B (WP-CLI): Run 'wp plugin update <plugin-name>' or 'wp plugin update --all'
If the plugin is no longer maintained, find an alternative or remove it
Test site functionality after updating

outdated-theme.title: Outdated WordPress Theme
outdated-theme.description: The active WordPress theme or installed themes are running outdated versions.
outdated-theme.impact: Vulnerable themes can allow attackers to inject malicious code, deface the website, or gain backdoor access.
outdated-theme.fix_summary: Update the affected theme(s) to the latest version.
fix_steps: BACKUP your site before updating
Option A (WP Admin): Go to Appearance > Themes > Click 'Update Now' on affected theme
Option B (WP-CLI): Run 'wp theme update <theme-name>' or 'wp theme update --all'
Remove any unused/inactive themes to reduce attack surface
Test site appearance and functionality after updating

xmlrpc-enabled.title: XML-RPC Endpoint Enabled
xmlrpc-enabled.description: The XML-RPC interface (xmlrpc.php) is accessible, which can be abused for brute-force attacks and DDoS amplification.
xmlrpc-enabled.impact: Attackers can perform credential brute-force attacks via XML-RPC's system.multicall method (much faster than wp-login) or use pingback for DDoS amplification.
xmlrpc-enabled.fix_summary: Disable XML-RPC if not needed, or restrict access.
fix_steps: Check if you need XML-RPC (used by mobile apps, Jetpack, some plugins)
Option A (.htaccess): Add a <Files xmlrpc.php> block with 'Order Deny,Allow' and 'Deny from all' directives
Option B (Plugin): Install and activate the 'Disable XML-RPC' plugin from the WordPress plugin directory
Option C (Nginx): Add 'location = /xmlrpc.php { deny all; }' to your server block
Verify by accessing https://yoursite.com/xmlrpc.php - it should return a 403 Forbidden response

directory-listing.title: Directory Listing Enabled
directory-listing.description: Web server directory listing is enabled, exposing file and directory structure to attackers.
directory-listing.impact: Attackers can browse directories to discover sensitive files, backup files, configuration files, or hidden admin pages.
directory-listing.fix_summary: Disable directory listing on the web server.
fix_steps: Option A (.htaccess): Add 'Options -Indexes' to your .htaccess file
Option B (Nginx): Ensure 'autoindex off;' is set in your server block
Option C (Apache conf): Set 'Options -Indexes' in the <Directory> directive
Verify by accessing a directory URL (e.g., /wp-content/uploads/) - it should return 403 Forbidden

missing-security-headers.title: Missing Security Headers
missing-security-headers.description: The web server is not sending one or more recommended security headers.
missing-security-headers.impact: Missing headers like X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, or Strict-Transport-Security leave the site vulnerable to clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and downgrade attacks.
missing-security-headers.fix_summary: Add the recommended security headers to your web server configuration.
fix_steps: Apache (.htaccess): Add 'Header set' directives for X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, X-XSS-Protection, Strict-Transport-Security, Content-Security-Policy, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy
Nginx (server block): Add corresponding 'add_header' directives with the 'always' flag for each header listed above
Test your headers at https://securityheaders.com to confirm they are applied correctly
Adjust Content-Security-Policy to match your site's needs (overly strict policies may break inline scripts or third-party resources)

sql-injection.title: SQL Injection Vulnerability
sql-injection.description: The application is vulnerable to SQL injection, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary SQL queries.
sql-injection.impact: Attackers can read, modify, or delete database contents, bypass authentication, or execute commands on the server.
sql-injection.fix_summary: Use parameterized queries, validate all user inputs, and deploy a WAF.
fix_steps: IMMEDIATE: Deploy a Web Application Firewall (WAF) like Wordfence or Sucuri to block SQLi attempts
Identify the vulnerable parameter/form from the scan results
In custom code: Replace direct SQL queries with WordPress $wpdb->prepare() method to use parameterized queries
If the vulnerability is in a plugin, update the plugin immediately or contact the developer
Validate and sanitize all user inputs using WordPress sanitization functions (sanitize_text_field, absint, etc.)
Apply the principle of least privilege to database user accounts

xss.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Vulnerability
xss.description: The application is vulnerable to cross-site scripting, allowing injection of malicious scripts.
xss.impact: Attackers can steal session cookies, redirect users to malicious sites, deface the website, or perform actions as the victim user.
xss.fix_summary: Escape all output, validate inputs, and implement Content-Security-Policy headers.
fix_steps: IMMEDIATE: Add Content-Security-Policy header to prevent inline script execution
Escape all dynamic output using WordPress functions: esc_html() for content, esc_attr() for attributes, esc_url() for URLs, esc_js() for JavaScript, wp_kses() for allowing specific HTML tags
Validate and sanitize all user inputs on the server side using sanitize_text_field(), wp_kses_post(), etc.
If the vulnerability is in a plugin or theme, update to the latest version immediately
Deploy a WAF with XSS protection rules enabled

weak-tls.title: Weak TLS/SSL Configuration
weak-tls.description: The server supports outdated or insecure TLS/SSL protocols or cipher suites.
weak-tls.impact: Attackers can intercept encrypted traffic via downgrade attacks (POODLE, BEAST) or brute-force weak ciphers.
weak-tls.fix_summary: Disable TLS 1.0/1.1 and weak ciphers; enable TLS 1.2+ with strong ciphers only.
fix_steps: Apache (ssl.conf): Set 'SSLProtocol -all +TLSv1.2 +TLSv1.3' and configure SSLCipherSuite with ECDHE-based ciphers, then set 'SSLHonorCipherOrder on'
Nginx: Set 'ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;' and configure ssl_ciphers with ECDHE-based cipher suites, then set 'ssl_prefer_server_ciphers on;'
Test your configuration at https://www.ssllabs.com/ssltest/ - aim for an A or A+ rating
Ensure HSTS header is set (see Missing Security Headers fix if applicable)
Restart the web server after applying changes

debug-mode.title: WordPress Debug Mode Enabled
debug-mode.description: WordPress debug mode (WP_DEBUG) is enabled on a production site, potentially exposing error messages and file paths.
debug-mode.impact: Debug messages can reveal sensitive information like file paths, database queries, plugin internals, and PHP errors to attackers.
debug-mode.fix_summary: Disable debug mode in wp-config.php for production.
fix_steps: Open wp-config.php on your server
Find and change: define('WP_DEBUG', true) to define('WP_DEBUG', false)
Also ensure WP_DEBUG_DISPLAY and WP_DEBUG_LOG are both set to false
Remove any debug.log files from the wp-content/ directory
If you need debugging, use a staging environment instead of production

file-editor-enabled.title: WordPress File Editor Enabled
file-editor-enabled.description: The built-in theme/plugin file editor in WordPress admin is enabled.
file-editor-enabled.impact: If an attacker gains admin access, they can modify PHP files directly from the dashboard to inject malware or create backdoors.
file-editor-enabled.fix_summary: Disable the file editor in wp-config.php.
fix_steps: Open wp-config.php on your server
Add define('DISALLOW_FILE_EDIT', true); before the line '/* That's all, stop editing! */'
Save the file and verify: In WP Admin, the 'Editor' option should no longer appear under Appearance or Plugins

default-admin-user.title: Default 'admin' Username Detected
default-admin-user.description: The default 'admin' username is in use, making brute-force attacks easier.
default-admin-user.impact: Attackers commonly target the 'admin' username in brute-force attempts. Using it halves the guessing work.
default-admin-user.fix_summary: Create a new administrator account with a unique username and delete the 'admin' account.
fix_steps: Log in to WP Admin as 'admin'
Go to Users > Add New > Create a new user with a unique username and Administrator role
Log out and log in as the new admin user
Go to Users > Delete the 'admin' user > Attribute all content to your new user
Alternatively via WP-CLI: wp user create newadmin email@example.com --role=administrator

weak-passwords.title: Weak Password Detected
weak-passwords.description: One or more user accounts have weak or commonly-used passwords.
weak-passwords.impact: Weak passwords can be cracked via brute-force or dictionary attacks, giving attackers full access to the affected account.
weak-passwords.fix_summary: Enforce strong passwords and enable two-factor authentication.
fix_steps: Force password reset for all affected accounts immediately
Install a password policy plugin (e.g., 'Melapress Login Security' or 'Password Policy Manager')
Set minimum requirements: 12+ characters, uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters
Install and enable Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) using a plugin like 'WP 2FA'
Limit login attempts with a plugin like 'Limit Login Attempts Reloaded'
Add CAPTCHA to the login page using a plugin like 'reCaptcha by BestWebSoft'

backup-files-exposed.title: Backup Files Exposed
backup-files-exposed.description: Backup files (.sql, .zip, .bak, .old, .tar.gz) are accessible from the web.
backup-files-exposed.impact: Exposed backup files can contain the entire database (including passwords), source code, and configuration files.
backup-files-exposed.fix_summary: Remove or restrict access to backup files.
fix_steps: IMMEDIATE: Remove exposed backup files from web-accessible directories
Move backups to a directory outside the web root (above public_html/)
Block access to backup file extensions in .htaccess using a <FilesMatch> directive for .sql, .bak, .old, .zip, .tar, .gz, .log, and .swp extensions
Use a backup plugin that stores backups off-site (e.g., UpdraftPlus with cloud storage like Google Drive or S3)
Set up automated backup cleanup policies to remove old local backups

csrf.title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) Vulnerability
csrf.description: The application does not properly validate CSRF tokens (nonces) in form submissions.
csrf.impact: Attackers can trick authenticated users into performing unintended actions like changing settings, creating accounts, or deleting content.
csrf.fix_summary: Implement WordPress nonce verification in all forms and AJAX requests.
fix_steps: Add nonce field to all forms using wp_nonce_field('action_name', 'nonce_name')
Verify nonce on form submission using check_admin_referer('action_name', 'nonce_name')
For AJAX requests, use wp_create_nonce('action_name') on the client side and check_ajax_referer('action_name') on the server side
If the vulnerability is in a plugin, update it or contact the developer with a responsible disclosure report
Never use $_GET or $_POST values without nonce verification for state-changing operations

information-disclosure.title: Information Disclosure
information-disclosure.description: The server is exposing version information, internal paths, or other sensitive details.
information-disclosure.impact: Disclosed information helps attackers fingerprint the server and find version-specific exploits.
information-disclosure.fix_summary: Hide version information and remove unnecessary exposure.
fix_steps: Hide WordPress version by adding remove_action('wp_head', 'wp_generator') to your theme's functions.php
Hide PHP version by setting 'expose_php = Off' in php.ini
Hide server version - Apache: add 'ServerTokens Prod' and 'ServerSignature Off' to httpd.conf; Nginx: add 'server_tokens off;' to nginx.conf
Remove readme.html and license.txt from the WordPress root directory
Block web access to wp-includes/version.php using .htaccess or Nginx rules

user-enumeration.title: User Enumeration Possible
user-enumeration.description: WordPress usernames can be discovered via author archives (?author=1) or the REST API (/wp-json/wp/v2/users).
user-enumeration.impact: Knowing valid usernames makes targeted brute-force and phishing attacks more effective.
user-enumeration.fix_summary: Restrict user enumeration via REST API and author archives.
fix_steps: Block author enumeration in .htaccess by adding a RewriteCond for 'author=' query strings with a RewriteRule to return 403
Restrict the REST API user endpoint by adding a filter on 'rest_endpoints' in functions.php to unset '/wp/v2/users' endpoints
Or install a security plugin that includes user enumeration protection (e.g., Wordfence, All In One WP Security)
